title: Insulating and draught-sealing a loft hatch
subtitle: Rigid insulation on the hatch lid, foam strip on the frame
excerpt: An uninsulated loft hatch is a cold rectangle in the ceiling. Adding a rigid insulation pad to the lid and foam strip to the frame takes an hour. It costs around and the payback is inside the first heating season.
sourceNotes: Energy Saving Trust loft insulation guidance. Loft hatch R-value improvement measured at around 1.0 m²·K/W when adding 100 mm PIR, bringing the hatch U-value from ~3.0 (uninsulated) to below 0.5.

content.text: The loft hatch is a cold spot in an otherwise insulated ceiling. Loft insulation stops at the hatch frame; the hatch itself is typically a sheet of 9 mm plywood or MDF with a U-value around 3.0 W/m²·K, roughly eight times worse than a well-insulated ceiling. Adding 100 mm of
PIR board
to the lid brings that below 0.5 W/m²·K and costs around in materials.
What you need
PIR insulation board, 100 mm thick, cut to the lid dimensions. Alternatively, stacked 50 mm sheets if cutting a thicker board is harder.
Foam or rubber draught strip, P-profile or E-profile for the hatch frame rebate.
Contact adhesive or expanding foam adhesive, for bonding PIR to the lid.
Tape measure and sharp utility knife or old saw for cutting PIR board.
Low-expansion foam for any gaps between hatch frame and ceiling.

attrs.body: Check the loft hatch for structural integrity before adding weight. A 100 mm PIR pad on a standard 600 × 600 mm hatch weighs about 1.8 kg, well within capacity. If the hatch is on a pull-down ladder, check the manufacturer's rated load; most are rated to 100 kg+ and the insulation pad weight is trivial.
attrs.title: Before you start

content.text: Insulating the hatch lid
Step 1, measure the lid.
Measure the loft hatch lid (not the frame opening). Note the length and width. If the lid sits in a rebate, measure the rebate so the insulation pad clears the frame when the hatch closes.
Step 2, cut the PIR board.

Score the foil face with a sharp utility knife and snap the board. A single score line and a clean snap gives a straight edge. Cut slightly undersize (5 mm less each way) so the pad doesn't jam against the frame.
Step 3, bond the pad.
Apply contact adhesive to both the board back and the lid, or use expanding foam adhesive in short beads. Press the pad onto the lid and hold for 30 seconds. Leave to cure before closing the hatch.
Draught-sealing the frame
Step 4, clean the frame rebate.
Wipe the rebate with a dry cloth to remove dust. Press P-profile or E-profile foam strip firmly along all four sides of the frame rebate, cutting cleanly at the corners.
Step 5, seal any gap around the frame.

Check the junction between the frame edge and the ceiling plaster. If there is a visible gap, run a thin bead of low-expansion foam or acrylic sealant around the perimeter. This is the second most common draught path after the hatch lid itself.

items.fix: Trim 5-10 mm from the pad edges with a utility knife.
items.cause: PIR pad cut too large and catching the frame
items.symptom: Hatch won't close flush after adding insulation

items.fix: Seal gap with low-expansion polyurethane foam or acrylic sealant.
items.cause: Gap between frame and ceiling plaster unfilled
items.symptom: Draught still felt around frame after strip fitted
